001.notes: Émis le 23 avril 1851; Qté : 250 200; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Premier timbre-poste du Canada, conçu par Sir Sandford Fleming. Le castor a été choisi comme symbole de l'ardeur au travail — premier timbre au monde à représenter un animal plutôt qu'un chef d'État. Les variétés sur papier vergé (vertical et horizontal) sont très recherchées.

002.notes: Émis le 12 mai 1851; Qté : 100 400; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Époux de la Reine Victoria; le tarif de 6d couvrait l'envoi d'une lettre vers la Grande-Bretagne. Existe sur papier vergé et vélin, la version sur papier vergé étant considérablement plus rare.

003.notes: Émis le 14 juin 1851; Qté : 51 000; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Le timbre classique le plus rare du Canada — le 12d noir couvrait l'envoi d'une lettre à triple poids vers la Grande-Bretagne. Environ 100 exemplaires oblitérés subsistent. Une paire notable s'est vendue plus de 500 000 $, ce qui en fait l'un des timbres canadiens les plus précieux.

004.notes: Émis le 1er avril 1852; Qté : 2 850 300; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Deuxième tirage du motif du castor sur papier vélin au lieu du papier vergé. De multiples nuances existent, du vermillon au rouge orangé, et les collectionneurs distinguent de nombreuses variétés de planche.

005.notes: Émis le 1er janvier 1855; Qté : 172 200; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Cartier est représenté comme le premier Européen à explorer le fleuve Saint-Laurent de 1534 à 1542. La double valeur faciale (10d colonial / 8d sterling) reflète les systèmes monétaires en vigueur à l'époque.

006.notes: Émis le 1er mars 1855; Qté : 250 078; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Version regravée du timbre sur papier vélin plus épais. La nuance pourpre rougeâtre le distingue du premier tirage en violet ardoise.

007.notes: Émis le 1er août 1857; Qté : 2 600 520; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Le tarif de 7½d (équivalent de 6d sterling) couvrait l'envoi d'une lettre simple vers la Grande-Bretagne par paquebot canadien. La couleur verte en fait l'une des émissions les plus distinctives de l'ère des Pence.

008.notes: Émis le 1er août 1857; Qté : 2 600 520; Dentelure : non dentelé; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Le ½d rose servait à l'affranchissement des journaux et circulaires. Étant l'émission de plus faible valeur de l'ère des Pence, ce timbre a été très utilisé et les exemplaires en bon état sont peu courants.

009.notes: Émis le 1er novembre 1858; Qté : 850 100; Dentelure : 11,75; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Premier timbre dentelé du Canada, marquant la transition depuis les émissions non dentelées. La nuance pourpre rougeâtre et la nouvelle dentelure le distinguent des tirages antérieurs du ½d.

010.notes: Émis le 1er janvier 1859; Qté : 449 900; Dentelure : 11,75; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Version dentelée du célèbre motif du castor. Ce timbre fait partie des dernières émissions avant le passage du Canada à la monnaie décimale plus tard en 1859.

011.notes: Émis le 1er janvier 1859; Qté : 70 000; Dentelure : 11,75; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Dernière émission dentelée en Pence de Prince Albert. Avec seulement 70 000 exemplaires imprimés, c'est l'un des plus rares de la série dentelée en Pence.

012.notes: Émis le 1er juillet 1859; Qté : 26 725 450; Dentelure : 11,75, 12 x 11,75, 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York. Premier timbre canadien en monnaie décimale. Plusieurs dentelures existent, et la nuance rose varie considérablement — les collectionneurs répertorient de nombreuses teintes distinctes.

013.notes: Émis le 1er janvier 1859; Qté : 449 900; Dentelure : 11,75; Imprimeur : Rawdon, Wright, Hatch & Edson. Dernier timbre-castor de l'ère des Pence avant la décimalisation. La couleur rose foncé le distingue des émissions antérieures du castor à 3d.

014.notes: Émis le 1er juillet 1859; Qté : 5 700 000; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York. Prince Albert de l'ère décimale en brun, contrastant avec le premier tirage en rose. Utilisé pour le tarif de lettre recommandée. Plusieurs variétés de nuances existent.

015.notes: Émis le 1er juillet 1859; Qté : 26 725 450; Dentelure : 11,75, 12 x 11,75, 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York.

015a.notes: Émis le 1er juillet 1859; Qté : 26 725 450; Dentelure : 11,75, 12 x 11,75, 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York.

016.notes: Émis le 1er juillet 1859; Qté : 599 999; Dentelure : 11,75, 12 x 11,75, 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York.

017.notes: Émis le 1er juillet 1859; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York. Premier tirage du 10¢ décimal Prince Albert en rose, remplacé ensuite par la nuance brune. La couleur rose est considérablement plus rare et très prisée des collectionneurs.

018.notes: Émis le 1er août 1864; Qté : 910 500; Dentelure : 11,75, 12 x 11,75, 12; Imprimeur : American Bank Note Company, New York.

019.notes: Émis le 1er avril 1868; Qté : 6 700 000; Dentelure : 12, 11,5 x 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Fait partie de la série des Grandes Reines — premiers timbres du Canada après la Confédération. La nuance jaune-vert existe sur papier filigrané et non filigrané.

019a.notes: Émis le 1er avril 1868; Qté : 6 700 000; Dentelure : 12, 11,5 x 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Variante de nuance bleu-vert de la Grande Reine à ½¢. Fait partie des premiers timbres émis après la Confédération de 1867.

020.notes: Émis le 1er avril 1868; Qté : 6 700 000; Dentelure : 12, 11,5 x 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

026.notes: Émis le 15 janvier 1870; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

028.notes: Émis le 1er janvier 1872; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Fait partie de la série des Petites Reines, qui a remplacé les Grandes Reines afin de réduire les coûts de production. Les Petites Reines ont été en usage pendant plus de 25 ans, ce qui en fait la plus longue série courante du Canada.

029.notes: Émis le 1er janvier 1872; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Émission des Petites Reines sur divers types de papier — les collectionneurs distinguent les tirages d'Ottawa et de Montréal selon la qualité du papier et les différences de nuances.

030.notes: Émis le 1er novembre 1874; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

031.notes: Émis le 1er octobre 1875; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Fait partie de la série des Petites Reines. La valeur de 5¢ couvrait le tarif de lettre domestique à taux simple. De nombreuses variétés de nuances et de types de papier existent.

032.notes: Émis le 15 novembre 1875; Qté : 29 387 500; Dentelure : 12, 12,25 x 12, 12 x 11,75, 12 x 11,5; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

033.notes: Émis le 15 novembre 1875; Qté : 29 387 500; Dentelure : 12, 12,25 x 12, 12 x 11,75, 12 x 11,5; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

034.notes: Émis le 15 novembre 1875; Qté : 29 387 500; Dentelure : 12, 12,25 x 12, 12 x 11,75, 12 x 11,5; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

035.notes: Émis le 1er février 1876; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company.

036.notes: Émis le 12 juillet 1882; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Fait partie de la série des Petites Reines. La valeur de 1¢, principalement utilisée pour les imprimés et les lettres locales, se trouve dans une vaste gamme de nuances, du jaune à l'orange foncé.

037.notes: Émis le 1er août 1893; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Le 20¢ Widow Weeds, ainsi nommé parce qu'il représente la Reine Victoria en tenue de deuil à la suite du décès du Prince Albert en 1861.

038.notes: Émis le 1er août 1893; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. La valeur de 50¢ de la série des Petites Reines. Principalement utilisée pour les colis plus lourds et les lettres recommandées vers des destinations étrangères.

039.notes: Émis le 1er août 1893; Dentelure : 12; Imprimeur : British American Bank Note Company. Fait partie de la série des Petites Reines. Ces tirages tardifs sur papier plus blanc se distinguent des tirages antérieurs de Montréal et d'Ottawa.

2180.notes: Émis le 26 mars 2004; Qté : 4 000 000; Dentelure : 13; Imprimeur : The three stamps in the souvenir set depict a portrait of Sverdrup, the Fram , and a landing boat approaching an Arctic island, possibly in that part of Nunavut now known as the Sverdrup Islands. All are printed in four-colour lithography with one colour gravure, based on images engraved by the Norwegian designer Martin Mörk, whose work is already familiar to Canadians-he created the polar bear engraving for the $2.00 definitive Canada Post issued in 1998.

3029.notes: Émis le 27 avril 2017; Qté : 90 000; Imprimeur : Lowe-Martin; Série : Star Trek. The Borg — the cybernetic collective that became one of Star Trek's most iconic antagonists, first introduced in The Next Generation.

3030.notes: Série : Formula 1 in Canada. Sir Jackie Stewart — three-time F1 World Champion who raced at the Circuit Gilles Villeneuve (then Île Notre-Dame) in Montreal.

3031.notes: Série : Formula 1 in Canada. Gilles Villeneuve (1950–1982) — Québec-born F1 driver and Canadian national hero; the Montreal circuit was renamed in his honour after his death.

3032.notes: Série : Formula 1 in Canada. Ayrton Senna — Brazilian legend who won the Canadian Grand Prix twice and is regarded as one of the greatest drivers in F1 history.

3033.notes: Série : Formula 1 in Canada. Michael Schumacher — seven-time World Champion who won a record seven Canadian Grands Prix in Montreal.

3034.notes: Série : Formula 1 in Canada. Lewis Hamilton — one of the most successful F1 drivers ever, with multiple wins at the Canadian Grand Prix in Montreal.

3035.notes: Canada's first Eid stamp, issued in 2017 to mark the Islamic celebrations of Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha, reflecting Canada's multicultural heritage.

3036.notes: Série : Canada 150. Expo 67 in Montreal — the World's Fair that celebrated Canada's centennial and became a defining cultural moment, attracting over 50 million visitors.

3038.notes: Série : Canada 150. The 1972 Summit Series between Canada and the Soviet Union — Paul Henderson's Game 8 winning goal remains one of the most iconic moments in Canadian sports history.

3039.notes: Série : Canada 150. Terry Fox's Marathon of Hope (1980) — the one-legged runner from Port Coquitlam, C.-B., ran 5 373 km across Canada to raise funds for cancer research before the disease forced him to stop.

3040.notes: Série : Canada 150. The Canadarm — Canada's robotic space arm used on 90 Space Shuttle missions; its successor, Canadarm2, operates on the International Space Station.

3043.notes: Série : Canada 150. Marriage equality — Canada became the fourth country in the world and the first in the Americas to legalize same-sex marriage in 2005.

3051.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada. Blue jay (Cyanocitta cristata) — an instantly recognizable species found across southern and central Canada, known for its bold blue plumage and loud calls.

3052.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada. Gyrfalcon (Falco rusticolus) — the world's largest falcon species, found across Canada's Arctic tundra. A prized bird in the history of falconry.

3053.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada. Common loon (Gavia immer) — Canada's iconic waterbird, featured on the one-dollar coin ('loonie'). Its haunting call is a quintessential sound of the Canadian wilderness.

3054.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada. Great gray owl (Strix nebulosa) — the largest owl in North America by length; inhabits Canada's boreal forests and is the provincial bird of Manitoba.

3055.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada. Osprey (Pandion haliaetus) — a fish-eating raptor found near waterways across Canada. Unlike most raptors, it dives feet-first to catch prey.

3122.notes: Série : Merveilles météorologiques (II). Steam fog — also called sea smoke, a phenomenon seen when cold Arctic air moves over warmer open water, common in Canada's maritime regions.

3123.notes: Série : Merveilles météorologiques (II). Waterspout — a tornado-like vortex over water, occasionally observed on the Great Lakes and coastal waters of Canada.

3124.notes: Série : Merveilles météorologiques (II). Lenticular clouds — lens-shaped formations that form near mountains, frequently observed over the Canadian Rockies.

3125.notes: Série : Merveilles météorologiques (II). Light pillars — vertical beams of light caused by ice crystals in the atmosphere, a striking winter phenomenon across the Canadian Prairies and North.

3126.notes: Série : Merveilles météorologiques (II). Moon halo — a ring of light around the moon caused by ice crystals in high cirrus clouds, commonly seen in Canadian winter skies.

3127.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada (II). Black-capped chickadee — the provincial bird of Nouveau-Brunswick and a familiar year-round resident across Canada, known for its distinctive 'chick-a-dee-dee-dee' call.

3128.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada (II). Whooping crane — one of North America's most endangered birds, with a wild population that migrates between Wood Buffalo National Park and Texas.

3129.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada (II). Canada goose — an iconic migratory bird; the V-shaped flying formations are a hallmark of Canadian autumn and spring.

3130.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada (II). Snowy owl — an Arctic species and Québec's provincial bird, which periodically irrupts southward into southern Canada in winter.

3131.notes: Série : Oiseaux du Canada (II). Steller's jay — Colombie-Britannique's provincial bird, found in the Pacific coast forests. Named after German naturalist Georg Steller.

3161.notes: Série : Canadiens en vol. Avro CF-105 Arrow — the legendary Canadian supersonic interceptor whose cancellation in 1959 remains one of the most debated decisions in Canadian aviation history.

3162.notes: Série : Canadiens en vol. Elsie MacGill (1905–1980) — the world's first woman to earn an aeronautical engineering degree, she oversaw production of the Hawker Hurricane at Canadian Car & Foundry during Seconde Guerre mondiale, earning the nickname 'Queen of the Hurricanes.'

3163.notes: Série : Canadiens en vol. William George Barker VC (1894–1930) — Canada's most decorated war hero of WWI, awarded the Victoria Cross for single-handedly engaging 60 enemy aircraft over France.

3164.notes: Série : Canadiens en vol. Ultraflight Lazair — a Canadian-designed ultralight aircraft created by Dale Kramer in the 1970s, considered one of the most successful ultralight designs ever produced.

3165.notes: Série : Canadiens en vol. C.H. 'Punch' Dickins (1899–1995) — a bush pilot who opened air routes across Canada's North. First to fly over the Barren Lands of the Territoires du Nord-Ouest.

3166.notes: Série : Doux Canada. Nanaimo bar — the no-bake, three-layered dessert bar from Nanaimo, C.-B., that has become one of Canada's most iconic treats.

3167.notes: Série : Doux Canada. Saskatoon berry pie — made from the native saskatoon berry (Amelanchier alnifolia), a staple food of Indigenous peoples of the Prairies.

3168.notes: Série : Doux Canada. Butter tart — a quintessentially Canadian pastry of butter, sugar, and eggs in a flaky shell. Its origins are debated among Ontario towns.

3169.notes: Série : Doux Canada. Sugar pie (tarte au sucre) — a traditional Québécois dessert made with brown sugar or maple syrup, deeply rooted in French-Canadian cuisine.

3170.notes: Série : Doux Canada. Blueberry grunt — a Maritime dessert of stewed blueberries topped with dumplings, named for the 'grunting' sound it makes while cooking.

3171.notes: Commemorates the Vancouver Asahi, a Japanese-Canadian baseball team (1914–1941) celebrated for their skilful 'brain ball' style of play. The team was disbanded when Japanese Canadians were interned during Seconde Guerre mondiale.

3256.notes: Série : Snow Mammals. Snowshoe hare — named for its large hind feet that act like snowshoes. Its fur changes from brown to white seasonally, a key adaptation for survival in Canadian winters.

3407.notes: Émis le 28 octobre 2024; With 'Farmerettes'. Soldiers of the Soil — young men and boys recruited to work on Canadian farms during WWI to address wartime labour shortages.

3408.notes: Émis le 4 novembre 2024. Série : Noël 2024. Traditional Nativity scene design for the religious holiday stamp.

3409.notes: Émis le 4 novembre 2024; Série : Noël — Gingerbread Houses. Permanent-rate holiday stamp featuring a whimsical gingerbread house design.

3410.notes: Émis le 4 novembre 2024; Série : Noël — Gingerbread Houses. Second gingerbread house design at the lettermail rate.

3411.notes: Émis le 4 novembre 2024; Série : Noël — Gingerbread Houses. Third gingerbread house design at the international rate.

3412.notes: Émis le 14 novembre 2024. Série : Hanukkah. Annual issue celebrating the Jewish festival of lights.

3413.notes: Émis le 27 novembre 2024; Série : French-Canadian Authors. Marie-Claire Blais (1939–2021) — Québec novelist whose debut Une saison dans la vie d'Emmanuel (1965) won the Prix Médicis and established her as a major voice in francophone literature.

3414.notes: Émis le 27 novembre 2024; Série : French-Canadian Authors. Jean Marc Dalpé (b. 1957) — Franco-Ontarian playwright, poet, and actor whose work explores working-class life and the Franco-Ontarian experience.

3415.notes: Émis le 27 novembre 2024; Série : French-Canadian Authors. Dany Laferrière (b. 1953) — Haitian-Canadian novelist elected to the Académie française in 2013, the first Haitian and Québécois member. Author of Comment faire l'amour avec un Nègre sans se fatiguer.

3416.notes: Émis le 27 novembre 2024; Série : French-Canadian Authors. Antonine Maillet (b. 1929) — Acadian novelist and playwright who won the Prix Goncourt in 1979 for Pélagie-la-Charrette, the first non-European to receive the award.

3417.notes: Émis le 27 novembre 2024; Série : French-Canadian Authors. Marguerite-A. Primeau (1914–2011) — pioneering Franco-Albertan author whose novels chronicled francophone life on the Canadian Prairies.

3418.mainTopic: Mois de l'histoire des Noirs — Marie-Joseph Angélique
3418.notes: Émis le 31 janvier 2025. Série : Histoire des Noirs Month. Marie-Joseph Angélique (c. 1705–1734) — an enslaved Black woman in Montreal who was accused of setting a fire that destroyed much of the city in 1734 and was executed. Her story has become a symbol of resistance to slavery in New France.

3419.notes: Émis le 3 mars 2025; Série : Flowers (Peonies). Peony 'Cytherea' — a coral-pink hybrid herbaceous peony, popular in Canadian perennial gardens for its reliability and fragrance.

3420.notes: Émis le 3 mars 2025; Série : Flowers (Peonies). Peony 'Marie-Victorin' — named after the Québec botanist Frère Marie-Victorin (1885–1944), who founded the Jardin botanique de Montréal.

3421.notes: Émis le 10 mars 2025. Série : Eid. Annual issue celebrating Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha.

3422.notes: Émis le 21 mars 2025. Commemorates Brian Mulroney (1939–2024), Canada's 18th Prime Minister (1984–1993), who introduced the GST, negotiated the Canada-U.S. Free Trade Agreement, and led the fight against apartheid in South Africa within the Commonwealth.

3423.notes: Émis le 4 avril 2025. Honours Blue Rodeo — one of Canada's most enduring and beloved rock-country bands, formed in Toronto in 1984. Inducted into the Canadian Music Hall of Fame.

3424.notes: Émis le 28 avril 2025; Semipostal to support community projects. Annual Canada Post Community Foundation semipostal stamp.

3425.notes: Émis le 30 mai 2025; Série : Places of Pride. Club Carousel in Calgary — a significant gathering place for the LGBTQ2+ community in Alberta's history.

3426.mainTopic: Places of Pride — Hanlan’s Point Gay Day Picnic (Toronto, 1971)
3426.notes: Émis le 30 mai 2025; Série : Places of Pride. Hanlan's Point on the Toronto Islands hosted Canada's first documented gay day picnic in 1971, a foundational moment in Canadian LGBTQ2+ history.

3427.mainTopic: Places of Pride — Truxx Bar Police Raid (Montreal, 1977)
3427.notes: Émis le 30 mai 2025; Série : Places of Pride. The 1977 police raid on the Truxx bar in Montreal galvanised Québec's LGBTQ2+ rights movement, often compared to New York's Stonewall uprising.

3428.mainTopic: Places of Pride — North American Native Gay & Lesbian Gathering (Beausejour, 1990)
3428.notes: Émis le 30 mai 2025; Série : Places of Pride. The 1990 North American Native Gay & Lesbian Gathering in Beausejour, Manitoba — a landmark event for Two-Spirit Indigenous people.

3429.notes: Émis le 20 juin 2025; Série : Leaders autochtones. Julia Haogak Ogina — an Inuvialuit leader dedicated to preserving cultural traditions and language in the Western Arctic.

3430.notes: Émis le 20 juin 2025; Série : Leaders autochtones. Bruce Starlight — Tsuu T'ina Nation leader and elder who worked to preserve Indigenous language and culture in southern Alberta.

3431.notes: Émis le 20 juin 2025; Série : Leaders autochtones. Sophie McDougall — an Indigenous community leader who contributed to cultural preservation and reconciliation efforts.

3432.notes: Émis le 17 juillet 2025. Acknowledges the civilian internment of various communities during the World Wars, including Ukrainian, Japanese, Italian, and German Canadians — a dark chapter in Canadian history now formally recognised.

3433.notes: Émis le Aug. 27, 2025; Série : Fungi. Star-tipped reindeer lichen (Cladonia stellaris) — a slow-growing lichen of Canada's boreal forests and tundra, an essential food source for caribou.

3434.notes: Émis le Aug. 27, 2025; Série : Fungi. Oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus) — a widely foraged edible species found on deadwood in Canadian forests; also cultivated commercially.

3435.mainTopic: Fungi — Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus)
3435.notes: Émis le Aug. 27, 2025; Série : Fungi. Chicken of the woods (Laetiporus sulphureus) — a brightly coloured bracket fungus prized by foragers for its meaty texture, found on hardwoods across Canada.

3436.notes: Émis le Aug. 27, 2025; Série : Fungi. Tinder fungus (Fomes fomentarius) — a hoof-shaped bracket fungus used since prehistoric times for starting fires. Found on birch and beech trees across Canada.

3437.notes: Émis le Aug. 27, 2025; Série : Fungi. Rhizophagus irregularis — a mycorrhizal fungus invisible to the naked eye, essential for plant nutrient uptake. Highlights the hidden importance of fungi in ecosystems.

3439.notes: Émis le Sept. 29, 2025 Se-tenant with #3438 and #3440. Booklet of 6. Depicts right panel of a Bentwood box

3440.notes: Émis le Sept. 29, 2025 Se-tenant with #3438 and #3439. Booklet of 6. Depicts left panel of a Bentwood box

3451.notes: Émis le Nov. 20, 2025 Booklet of 12. Série : Canadian Graphic Novelists (II). Kate Beaton — Cape Breton, Nouvelle-Écosse, cartoonist known for her webcomic Hark! A Vagrant and the Governor General's Award-winning graphic novel Ducks: Two Years in the Oil Sands.

3452.notes: Émis le Nov. 20, 2025 Booklet of 12. Série : Canadian Graphic Novelists (II). Jimmy Beaulieu — Québec comics artist and editor who has been a driving force in the province's indie bande dessinée scene.

3453.notes: Émis le Nov. 20, 2025 Booklet of 12. Série : Canadian Graphic Novelists (II). Guy Delisle — Québec cartoonist known for his travelogue comics set in North Korea (Pyongyang), Burma, and Jerusalem, blending memoir with political observation.

3454.notes: Émis le Nov. 20, 2025 Booklet of 12. Série : Canadian Graphic Novelists (II). Julie Doucet — Montreal underground comics pioneer known for her raw, influential autobiographical zine Dirty Plotte.

3455.mainTopic: Canadian Graphic Novelists — Bryan Lee O'Malley
3455.notes: Émis le Nov. 20, 2025 Booklet of 12. Série : Canadian Graphic Novelists (II). Bryan Lee O'Malley — London, Ontario, creator of the Scott Pilgrim series, which became a cult-classic graphic novel, film, and video game franchise.

3456.mainTopic: Canadian Graphic Novelists — Michael Nicoll Yahgulanaas
3456.notes: Émis le Nov. 20, 2025 Booklet of 12. Série : Canadian Graphic Novelists (II). Michael Nicoll Yahgulanaas — Haida artist who created the 'Haida Manga' genre, blending Haida formline art with Japanese manga storytelling.

3457.mainTopic: Mois de l'histoire des Noirs — Maestro Fresh Wes
3457.notes: Émis le Jan. 28, 2026; Hip-Hop in Canada series. Booklet of 6. Maestro Fresh Wes — Toronto rapper whose 1989 single 'Let Your Backbone Slide' is considered the first Canadian hip-hop hit and a landmark in the genre's Canadian history.

3458.mainTopic: Mois de l'histoire des Noirs — Michie Mee
3458.notes: Émis le Jan. 28, 2026; Hip-Hop in Canada series. Booklet of 6. Michie Mee — one of Canada's first female hip-hop artists, a Jamaican-Canadian rapper from Toronto who broke barriers in the late 1980s.

3459.mainTopic: Mois de l'histoire des Noirs — Muzion
3459.notes: Émis le Jan. 28, 2026; Hip-Hop in Canada series. Booklet of 6. Muzion — a pioneering Montreal hip-hop group from the late 1990s who helped establish francophone rap in Québec.

3460.notes: Émis le 2 mars 2026; Série : Spring Flowers. Pink cherry blossoms — celebrating the ornamental cherry trees that bloom across Canadian cities each spring, notably in Vancouver's public parks.

3461.notes: Émis le 2 mars 2026; Série : Spring Flowers. White cherry blossoms — Japan gifted cherry trees to several Canadian cities as symbols of friendship; Vancouver's Cherry Blossom Festival is a major annual event.

3462.notes: Expected: mars 2026. Série : Eid. Annual issue célébrant Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha.

3463.notes: Expected: Winter/Spring 2026. Celebrates maple syrup season — Canada produces approximately 75% of the world's maple syrup, predominantly in Québec, Ontario, and Nouveau-Brunswick.

3464.notes: Expected: Spring 2026; Série : Places of Pride. Continuing the series honouring significant places in Canadian LGBTQ2+ history.

3465.notes: Expected: juillet 2026. Marks the centennial of the Royal Canadian Legion, founded in 1926 to support veterans and their families and to keep alive the tradition of remembrance.

3466.mainTopic: National Day for Vérité et réconciliation
3466.notes: Expected: septembre 2026. National Day for Truth and Reconciliation — established as a federal statutory holiday on septembre 30 in 2021, honouring residential school survivors, their families, and their communities.

3467.notes: Expected: Fall 2026. Jack-o'-lanterns for the Halloween season — continuing Canada Post's tradition of festive seasonal stamps.

3468.notes: Expected: novembre 2026. Série : Remembrance Day. Honouring the sacrifices of Canadian servicemen and servicewomen in conflicts from WWI to modern peacekeeping missions.

3469.notes: Expected: 2026; Semipostal stamp. Annual Canada Post Community Foundation semipostal supporting programs for children and youth.

3470.notes: Expected: 2026. Wolves — the grey wolf (Canis lupus) remains widespread across Canada's boreal forests, tundra, and Rocky Mountains, playing a vital role as an apex predator.

3471.notes: Expected: 2026. Promotes blood donation awareness in Canada. Canadian Blood Services relies on voluntary donors to maintain the national blood supply.

3473.notes: Expected: 2026. Série : Hanukkah. Annual issue célébrant the Jewish festival of lights.
